The valuation difference is pronounced when I compare Viasat with companies such as CommScope (COMM), Calix (CALX), and Extreme Networks (EXTR).

Viasat trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 6x.

When we look across its peers, COMM, CALX, and EXTR trade at 9.8x, 12.9x, and 31.5x, respectively.

This suggests that Viasat is not only slightly cheaper, but it trades at less than half the peer average.

We see a similar trend in forward sales multiples. Viasat EV/S multiple is at 2x, which is pretty close to COMM and EXTR, but it is still way lower compared to Calix, which is more than 3.5x.

The discount becomes clearer when we look at the trailing multiples.

Viasat trades at a low P/CF of 3.4x. CommScope is almost 12x, Extreme is just over 17x, and Calix is at 42x.

Additionally, the P/S and P/B ratios also show how wide the gap actually is. VSAT’s P/S and P/B ratios are at 0.74x and 0.76x, respectively. On the other hand, its peers are trading at much higher valuations.

This indicates that the market is not ignoring Viasat but is significantly discounting it.

Viasat Valuation Path: Opportunities and Risks

For a heavy-spending business like Viasat, EV/EBITDA and cash-flow multiples are the relevant lenses. In the meantime, P/E is not a good guide here. Intensive depreciation and amortization, coupled with significant interest expenses, leave pre-tax earnings modest in the near term. That is why we can see the non-GAAP P/E of ~337x on FY2 estimates. Such a figure is mostly noise in this model.

A reference to the current market facts keeps the story concrete: currently at ~$26.10 and 134.3 million shares, the company’s market value is roughly $3.5 billion.

Enterprise value is $9.1 billion and net debt is $5.6 billion. That suggests an efficient forward EBITDA of $1.52 billion (EV ÷ 6x).

In other words, every per unit of movement in EV/EBITDA is equivalent to $1.52 billion of EV, or $11.3 per share, so incremental changes in the multiple have massive implications on equity value.

Due to that sensitivity, execution has become the de facto measure of valuation. Viasat, with a steady EBITDA, a well-managed capex, and visibly lowered debt, would generate upside even with modest multiple growth.

For example, assume the EBITDA is constant at the implied ~9x multiple of $60 per share. Alternatively, assuming the free cash flow stabilizes at ~$0.5 to 0.7B after capex and the market begins to use a P/FCF ratio of ~15x, the market capitalization will be ~$10 billion (= $74.5 per share).

Such scenarios clarify the mechanics: Cash conversion and visible deleveraging are the necessary stimuli to a re-rating.

On the other hand, the drawback is straightforward too.

A 5.5x multiple, based on the same EBITDA, would reduce EV to ~$8.34 billion and equity to ~$2.74 billion, or a share price of about $20.

A further squeeze to 5x brings the stock to the teens. In brief, modest shifts in the speed of conversion of cash or investor attitude towards leverage have large share-price effects.

Two clear catalysts can alter the math materially, as already discussed in the piece above.

The first is the ViaSat-3 satellite program, which will increase the network capacity of the company by more than twice.

The second satellite (F2) is under preparation for shipment, and the third (F3) is under environmental testing.

If launches and deployments continue to go according to schedule, Viasat will have an opportunity to generate new revenue at high incremental margins. Any delays would set growth back even more as costs keep running.

Then there is the Ligado agreement. If the court approves, it would authorize a maximum of $568 million in FY2026, in two payments, beginning in September.

This would cut net debt by around 10%. At a 7x EV/EBITDA re-rating, that alone would be worth $4-5 per share (568 million ÷ 134.3 million = ~ 4.2).

The effect of management guidance excluding Ligado proceeds is that any court approval would be an add-on upside to the above scenarios.

Bottom Line

The real investor question is not whether 6x is cheap or expensive in a vacuum, but whether Viasat can achieve a multiple-quarter pattern of (1) stability or increased adjusted EBITDA, (2) repeatable free cash flow as capex normalizes, and (3) debt paydown evidence.

Achieving all of this is pretty challenging in the near term.

When these things occur, the multiple math will take care of the rest. Until then, the stock will remain highly sensitive to both progress and setbacks.

The stock has good growth potential, but it is extremely contingent on execution.

It will not be an instant process to experience the stable free cash flow and considerable debt reduction due to high capex and constant project risks.

At this point, a Hold rating is reasonable, as the company deserves to have time to show that it can provide consistent performance.

data.title: My #1 Place To Buy Undervalued Income - And 3 Picks I'm Betting On Now
data.description:
Introduction

From a real estate standpoint, he says, there’s no better bet than America. “It doesn’t matter the administration, doesn’t matter the president—it’s always the US.

I was absolutely fascinated when I read the quote above in a recent article published by Bloomberg, as it basically reflects how I feel about investing in the United States in general.

As most of my readers may know, I have 100% of my portfolio invested in North American stocks, with the overwhelming majority of that capital being invested in the U.S., a market I always preferred over non-U.S. markets due to factors I highlighted in an article titled "Red, White, And Wealth: Building An All-American Dividend Portfolio."

While the U.S. housing market, in general, has hit a wall of affordability issues, including elevated rates, high home prices, and poor consumer sentiment, foreign buyers are rushing back, making 2025 the first year of higher foreign real estate investments since 2017, as we can see in the chart below.

Although foreign buyers account for only 2.5% of existing real estate housing deals, they are an increasing force, supported by less competition from U.S. buyers, as most foreign buyers are all-cash buyers who are, on average, in better financial shape than American buyers.

After all, someone who deliberately invests overseas is very different from an "average" American who's simply looking to buy a house for their family.

With that said, although there are certainly reasons not to invest in real estate, I believe the poor sentiment creates tremendous opportunities, as I wrote in an article titled "The Only 2 Places I'd Put Big Money For Income And Growth Right Now."

I brought up three reasons to consider real estate investments, all of which are related in some way:

1. Recent years were way below average

As we can see below, the past few years were quite bad for REITs, as the average annual return for the 2020-2024 period was just 5.5%, a mile below the 12.4% average annual return going back to the early 1990s.

While this doesn't mean we're in for a sudden rebound, it certainly indicates that the odds for successful investments are in our favor, as REITs have never had a period of subdued returns for more than four years.

2. Valuations are very attractive

Currently, REIT equities are trading at the biggest discount (6.5x) to the S&P 500 since the Great Financial Crisis, if we exclude the pandemic. Ever since inflation peaked at the end of 2022, it has been nonstop underperformance for REITs, as they simply could not keep up with the tech-heavy S&P 500. Besides that, the high rates on risk-free government bonds caused a lot of capital to flow into bonds instead of REITs, which are also income-focused investments.

Generally speaking, REITs do not trade at a huge discount to the market.

3. Cheap valuations don't last

The average total return following a period of REIT discounts of more than -2.0x (the current discount is 6.5x) is substantially higher for REITs than U.S. equities, in general, as we can see below. Looking back two decades, we can expect at least two years of REIT outperformance, technically speaking.

But wait, there's more!

In a market with a rather lofty valuation, real estate can function as a great alternative, as it has a very low correlation to U.S. stocks. While even value and small-cap stocks have a long-term correlation of more than 0.85, REITs have a correlation of roughly 0.65, making them excellent diversification tools.

Moreover, despite rising rates, REITs have done tremendously well, as their net operating income has consistently made new highs this year.

With that said, in this market, a few income-focused areas stand out to me.

One of them is the energy midstream industry, which benefits from fixed-fee contracts from pipeline and related support companies. I absolutely love that industry, as I am bullish on both energy supply and demand and the need to build new infrastructure in areas like the Permian Basin.

However, REITs have a benefit that midstream companies do not have, which is a better valuation and a history of consistency. See, while subdued oil prices do not hurt most midstream companies, whenever oil prices drop, investors tend to believe output will fall, which sometimes leads to steep stock price declines.

That's why I decided to highlight three of my favorite opportunities in the REIT space of companies with stellar balance sheets, excellent operations, and clear strategic benefits that clearly show the benefits of owning American real estate.

See, the goal isn't just to present three of my favorite REITs, but also to highlight the benefits that some REITs present and their attractive valuations.

I am also using the opportunity to discuss three super important developments in the real estate world.

So, even if you don't care about my picks, I am sure you'll get some usable data from this article.

Now, let's get to the picks!

VICI Properties (VICI) - When Income Meets Resilience In A Very Tough Market

Las Vegas is in trouble. At least for now.

The city is dealing with the steepest decline in visitor numbers since the Great Financial Crisis, as the chart below shows quite well.

According to the latest numbers from the Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority, June saw 11.3% fewer visitors, 10.7% lower convention attendance, a 6.5 points drop in hotel occupancy rates despite fewer available rooms (i.e., the closure of the Mirage and Tropicana), and a decline of nearly 14.0% in revenue per available room.

Also, looking at the charts in the overview below, it's fair to say that things have gone south more quickly.

On top of that, its real estate market is being pressured. Below is a screenshot from Zillow I posted on X (formerly known as Twitter) the other day. It shows homes available for sale in Summerlin, which is one of the fancier places in the greater Las Vegas area. I went with the caption "It looks like the entire city of Summerlin, Nevada, is for sale."

I'm not bringing this up to trash Las Vegas, but because I believe this city has become the epicenter of America's problems.

  • The housing market has reached a tipping point, as red-hot demand since the pandemic is fading, and sellers are finding fewer buyers. In Las Vegas, it's fair to assume the impact is bigger, as the market saw a large influx of buyers after 2021, fueled by issues in Southern California and tax advantages. In general, many people wanted a piece of the LV market.
  • General consumer sentiment is down. As Las Vegas Strip resorts have gone from offering cheap drinks, rooms, and food to attract gamblers to focusing on margins on most major products, affordability is down. People simply don't want to pay $20 for a cocktail on the strip or $25 for fish and chips.
  • The ongoing trade war and stricter visa regulations may have kept some visitors from traveling to the U.S., although the data on this is thin.

The interesting thing is that despite these headwinds, the resilience of high-quality REITs is undeniable.

VICI Properties is a Las Vegas-focused REIT, as it generates roughly half of its rents in Sin City, where it owns assets like MGM Grand, Caesars Palace, Mandalay Bay, Park MGM, The Venetian, and many others.

As we can see below, the overwhelming majority of its rent comes from Caesars Entertainment (CZR), MGM Resorts (MGM), and The Venetian, which is managed by Apollo Global Management (APO), a major private lender that has found value in America's biggest gambling and entertainment market.

While Las Vegas may have its issues, the risks for VICI remain low, as its mega tenants won't close resorts just to adjust to a temporary decline in demand.

Caesars and MGM, for example, pay a combined rent bill of more than $2.2 billion every single year. They rely on their resorts. It's not like a small donut shop that can move to a different part of town if it suddenly wants to.

Moreover, VICI has average lease terms of roughly 40 years, 100% occupancy, almost every penny of rent income protected by CPI-linked boosters, and highly differentiated assets.

During its 2Q25 earnings call, VICI noted that high-end properties maintain over 90% occupancy, with operators adjusting to attract budget-conscious consumers. I expect this to continue, as the margin-focused approach simply didn't work for operators.

The company also highlighted Las Vegas's evolution toward experiential offerings like day clubs, elevated dining, sports, and innovations targeting millennials and Gen Z, who now dominate visitor share.

All of this will take time, but Las Vegas will be fine. What matters is that while these adjustments may hurt operators, they won't hurt VICI Properties.

Moreover, VICI has a balance sheet with a 5.1x leverage ratio, close to the lower bound of its 5.0x-5.5x target range, supported by $2.9 billion in liquidity and a favorable outlook, as the company raised its per-share 2025 AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) target range to $2.35-$2.37 (from $2.33 to $2.36). This shows the benefits of owning high-quality real estate even in a tough market.

It has an investment-grade credit rating of BBB-.

Currently yielding 5.4%, the company has a five-year CAGR of 7.8%, protected by a 73% payout ratio.

Going forward, analysts see 3-4% annual per-share AFFO growth. When adding its dividend and the fact that VICI trades at 14.0x AFFO, roughly 1.7 points below its long-term average, I see a path to 13-14% annual returns if REITs start to rebound.

Without valuation tailwinds, it could still return nearly 10%.

Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) - Housing Has Issues, This REIT Does Not

Housing has major issues in the U.S. It's not just Summerlin in Nevada.

The aforementioned mix of high home prices, elevated rates, and poor consumer sentiment has made buying a house a far-fetched dream for many.

As we can see below, housing affordability is even worse than during the onset of the Great Financial Crisis. This has pushed the median age of a first-time homebuyer to nearly 39 years. Two decades ago, half of all buyers had closed their first deal before turning 32.

Despite supply shortages, homebuilder sentiment has hit a three-year low, as macroeconomic developments have become a huge burden. Needless to say, the longer the new housing supply remains subdued, the harder it becomes to improve affordability.

This is great for Equity LifeStyle Properties, a REIT that has been very high on my watchlist for years, as I believe the downtrend in REITs makes ELS one of the best long-term investments an investor can make.

I consider ELS a superior housing REIT, as it owns high-quality manufactured housing ("MH") communities, most of which are age-restricted. It also owns RV communities and marinas, all of which enjoy favorable supply and secular tailwinds. Its total asset base covers 455 properties and roughly 173,000 sites.

Even better, the constrained supply of new MH sites supports the value of ELS's communities, especially in high-demand markets like Florida, California, and Arizona, where the company has seen consistent home sales and high occupancy rates of more than 94%.

The overwhelming majority of its "tenants" own MH houses, which means they just pay monthly fees and take care of their own assets. This is favorable for ELS, as it lowers tenant turnover and makes maintenance much easier.

It also helps that the average monthly costs of an MH asset are 66% lower than those of a single-family home, mainly due to lower housing expenses. While an MH community isn't a suitable option for every potential SFH buyer, it is a market that has seen significant improvements in quality and notable cost advantages.

This also explains why the company has never had a negative year of NOI growth and favorable inflation protection that has allowed it to generate above-inflation NOI in every macro environment since at least 2001.

Thanks to efficient management, the company has grown its per-share AFFO by 8% since 2006. During this period, it has boosted its dividend by 20% per year. The cherry on top is that the average REIT grew its per-share CAGR of just 3.8% during this period.

Currently yielding 3.5%, the REIT has a five-year dividend CAGR of 8.9% and a 79% payout ratio. Looking ahead, analysts expect AFFO to accelerate to 6% per share growth in 2027, implying a 10% annual return without valuation benefits.

Although the REIT has underperformed its peers since the pandemic, I expect a bottom in the relative performance and long-term alpha in the years ahead.

The next REIT is a bit more cyclical, yet no less impressive.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) - The Industrial Real Estate Comeback

Currently, Rexford is my only REIT holding, a stock I bought a lot more of during the April sell-off, when I was able to add at less than $32 per share.

As I have written in prior articles, REXR stands out to me due to its "mission-critical" footprint in Southern California, where it owns 100% of its 422 industrial properties, giving it a market share of roughly 3% and a lot of room for off-market deals and value-adding projects due to the market's elevated average building age.

Although some people like to avoid SoCal for political reasons, I decided to mainly focus on its benefits, which include one of the best supply situations in all of North America, as SoCal is wedged between the ocean and mountains.

It is also home to America's biggest consumer base, its largest industrial market, and the San Pedro Port Complex, which handles close to a third of all U.S. port volumes, most of which remain in SocCal.

Rexford caters to companies focused on keeping SoCal's economy running, including warehouses, trade companies, general e-commerce, aerospace, manufacturing, defense, healthcare, and more.

Unfortunately, its stock price has been in a steep downtrend since

This downtrend was fueled by rising rates, new supply, and general economic worries. Even worse, this year's trade worries caused investors to price in tremendous weakness in SoCal's economy due to dependence on trade.

While I didn't enjoy these headwinds, I used them to boost my position.

Now, it seems investors are being rewarded.

While market rents across the company's portfolio declined by 12.8% year-over-year in the second quarter, the market is beginning to find a bottom thanks to easing supply headwinds. Currently, leasing activity accounts for 80% of the company's vacant space, up from 60% in 2024.

Cushman & Wakefield agrees, as their outlook sees a bottom in pricing and vacancy rates:

Vacancy increases will be modest, peaking in the high-7% range by 2026 before improving in 2027. Slower speculative construction will help limit vacancy growth, with 2025 deliveries down 46% from 2024 and falling further in 2026.

Rent growth is expected to decelerate, dipping below 2% by late 2025. As market conditions improve and supply and demand rebalance in late 2026, rent growth is projected to rebound toward the historical 3–4% range. - Cushman & Wakefield

And even before an upswing, Rexford remains in a good spot, as embedded growth remains strong at $195 million in incremental cash NOI, which is the equivalent to 28% growth, with $105 million coming from contractual rent steps, $70 million from ongoing repositioning and redevelopment projects, and $20 million from a 3% cash mark-to-market.

And, just like the other two REITs in this article, the balance sheet is strong, with $1.8 billion in liquidity, including $560 million in cash, and net debt to EBITDA at just 4.0x. It has a BBB+ credit rating, one step below the A range.

It currently yields 4.4%. This dividend has a five-year CAGR of 16.2%. Although I expect dividend growth to remain in the mid-single-digit range.

The company, which has an 81% payout ratio, is expected to grow its per-share AFFO by 3-5% per year in 2025-2027. I expect substantially higher growth if we see higher inflation (mark-to-market benefits) and broader economic growth (real estate demand).

Applying a 25x multiple, in line with peer multiples like Prologis (PLD), I see a fair stock price of at least $55-$60, roughly 40-50% above the current price.

Given the bottoming process in industrial real estate and REITs, in general, I will remain a buyer of REXR shares on weakness.

Takeaway

While the headlines scream about affordability crises and poor sentiment, I see a tremendous opportunity in what has become my favorite place to buy safe income for the next decade (and beyond).

To me, the data is clear.

Foreign investors are rushing back into U.S. real estate for a reason, and REITs are historically cheap, trading at their biggest discount to the market since the Financial Crisis.

I think it's fair to say this isn't just a statistical anomaly but a great setup for a period of potential outperformance.

The picks in this article each have a different facet of American real estate resilience, including irreplaceable assets, secular housing demand, and a mission-critical industrial footprint.

The goal isn't to time the bottom but to recognize that when high-quality assets with stellar balance sheets are on sale, the long-term odds are in our favor.

Risks To My Thesis

While all REITs mentioned in this article are "safe," the biggest risks are a steep recession for Rexford, as its occupancy rate risks are much higher than those of ELS and VICI.

In general, the biggest risk is prolonged underperformance if longer-term government bond rates do not come down.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Snatch Up This 7%-Yielding Aristocrat
data.description:
I like to own businesses with a corporate culture that rewards its investors.

That almost goes without saying, right? If I can't extract reliable and growing cash flow from a business, there's no point in owning it.

Over the years, I have found that an added characteristic of the most reliable companies is that they tend to have meaningful insider ownership.

Any executive team is probably going to want to keep their jobs. The best ones, though, are going to care all the more for a different reason. Their lives and substantial portions of their net worth are invested in the companies that they run.

These executive teams are going to be more apt to focus on doing what's right for the long run. That's because management and investor interests are perfectly aligned in such a scenario.

This brings me to Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD). The founding family/insiders own 32% of the common units (slide 4 of 47 of EPD's August 2025 Investor Deck). Talk about a vested interest in doing right by unitholders. This is why it's not a surprise the company has upped its distribution for 27 consecutive years (pretty much my entire life).

When I last covered EPD with a buy rating in May, I thought the company had decent growth potential ahead. EPD had the strongest balance sheet in the midstream industry. Units were also undervalued by a double-digit percentage.

Several months later, I'm reaffirming my buy rating. EPD expects $6 billion of projects to come online between now and the end of 2026. The company's liquidity and leverage ratio remain the envy of the industry. Topping off the buy case, EPD's units remain moderately undervalued.

More Growth Left In The Tank

On July 28th, EPD shared its earnings report for the second quarter ended June 30th, 2025. The company's revenue decreased by 15.7% year-over-year to $11.36 billion during the quarter. This fell $2.82 billion short of Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter.

What was behind the decline in the topline for the second quarter?

The gist of it was reduced marketing revenues. That stemmed from lower average sales prices for energy commodities during the second quarter. Global demand concerns from the trade war and supply headwinds led WTI crude oil to fall by 20.7% from an average price per barrel of $80.57 in Q2 2024 to $63.87 in Q2 2025.

Lower energy commodity prices were only partially offset by higher volumes in the second quarter. During the second quarter, Co-CEO Jim Teague noted in his opening remarks on the Q2 2025 Earnings Call that EPD set five volumetric records. Overall, the company's equivalent pipeline transportation volumes rose by about 6% to 13.6 million barrels per day.

EPD's volume growth was driven by two processing plants placed into service in the Permian in recent months. The company's $950 million all-cash deal for Piñon Midstream last October was another contributing factor to this volume growth.

Despite challenges to the topline, EPD executed well enough for the second quarter to log adjusted EBITDA and DCF growth. The former edged 0.8% higher over the year-ago period to $2.41 billion. The latter grew by 7% year-over-year to $1.94 billion.

In the years to come, EPD looks poised to keep growing.

For one, the company just placed its Orion and Mentone West gas processing plants into service. That added another 600 million cubic feet per day of gas processing capacity to EPD's energy infrastructure network.

Secondly, the company still has $6 billion in major capital projects under construction. These include many of the projects that I have discussed in past articles, such as the Mentone West 2 gas processing plant and the expansion of LPG loading capacity at the Enterprise Hydrocarbons Terminal.

The one notable addition to the company's growth project backlog is the 300 million cubic feet per day gas processing plant called Athena. This is set to be built in the Permian and expected to come online in Q4 2026. That investment opportunity arose from EPD's strategic acquisition of a gathering affiliate of Occidental earlier this month for $580 million in cash.

For these reasons, I believe that the FAST Graphs analyst growth consensus for OCF per unit is realistic. In 2025, the consensus is for OCF per unit to jump 6.9% to $4. For 2026, another 3.6% rise in OCF per unit to $4.14 is being anticipated. In 2027, an additional 2.6% increase in OCF per unit to $4.25 is the current forecast.

EPD's balance sheet also remains the undisputed heavyweight champion of its industry. As of June 30th, 2025, the company's leverage ratio was 3.1x, solidly within its targeted range of between 2.75x and 3.25x.

At the end of Q2 2025, EPD also held $5.1 billion in liquidity. The vast majority of the company's debt load was classified as fixed-rate debt (98%), with a 4.7% weighted-average cost of debt.

This points to a very low cost of capital for EPD, which looks like it can continue. In June, the company flexed its industry-leading A- S&P credit rating to secure $2 billion in financing via senior notes.

This was broken down into $500 million of notes due in June 2028 (4.3% fixed-rate coupon), $750 million in notes due January 2031 (5.2% fixed-rate coupon), and $750 million in notes due January 2036 (5.2% fixed-rate coupon). At the time of the pricing, the last tranche of notes was priced at a sub-100 basis point spread to the 4.4% yield of U.S. 10-year Treasury (US10Y) bonds (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to EPD's Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release, EPD's Q2 2025 10-Q Filing, and EPD's August 2025 Investor Deck).

Units Remain Attractively Valued

Since May, EPD's units have returned 8%. Over that same time, the S&P 500 index (SP500) has returned 14%. Even after these solid returns from the MLP, units arguably still offer plenty of value.

EPD's forward 12-month P/OCF ratio is just 7.7. For more context, this is below the 10-year average P/OCF ratio of 9.6, per FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, I'm sticking with my fair value P/OCF ratio of 8.6. As was the case in my previous article, that would be one standard deviation under the 10-year average P/OCF ratio.

EPD's annual forward OCF per unit growth consensus of 4.4% remains similar to the 10-year average annual OCF per unit growth rate of 5.8%. Along with a 5.4% annual DCF per unit growth consensus, this suggests that the company's growth profile is intact.

Once again, the interest rate environment will likely prevent EPD from re-rating to a P/OCF ratio of 9.6 (while still being fairly valued, anyway). The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield of 4.3% is well above the 10-year average of around 2.5%, according to data from Macrotrends.

In the years ahead, I expect the 10-year yield will average in the high 3% range. EPD has historically traded at an approximate 800 basis point spread to the 10-year yield. Applying this same spread to my forecast for the 10-year yield, that's how I get my fair value P/OCF ratio of 8.6.

As of now, the calendar year 2025 is 64% finished. This implies that another 36% of 2025 and 64% of 2026 are still to come in the next 12 months. That yields a forward 12-month OCF per unit of $4.09.

Plugging this in with my fair value P/OCF ratio of 8.6, I come out at a fair value of $35 a unit. Relative to the current $32 share price (as of August 21st, 2025), this works out to be a 10% discount to fair value.

If EPD returns to fair value and meets growth expectations, it would have a 19% upside by the end of September 2026. Through 2030, the partnership's annual total return potential would be more than 11%.

Distribution Growth Could Slightly Accelerate

EPD's 6.9% forward distribution yield remains respectable for the midstream industry. This starting income is meaningfully higher than all publicly traded C-Corp midstream companies, like Enbridge (ENB) and ONEOK (OKE). Yet, it's lower than MLP counterparts like Energy Transfer (ET) and MPLX LP (MPLX). This yield is above the energy sector median forward yield of 4.5%. This is enough for Seeking Alpha's Quant System to issue respective B+ and B grades for forward distribution yield and overall distribution yield (the latter is up from a B- grade in May).

Further adding to EPD's appeal as an income vehicle is the impressive reliability that I alluded to at the article's outset. The partnership's 27-year distribution growth streak is leaps and bounds better than the sector median of 2 years. That warrants an A+ grade from the Quant System for overall distribution consistency (unchanged from May).

EPD's distribution is also very viable for the future. In the first half of 2025, the partnership generated more than $3.9 billion in DCF. Against ~$2.3 billion in distributions paid to unitholders, this left the company with approximately $1.6 billion in retained DCF.

That equates to a DCF coverage ratio of 1.7 (or a payout ratio in the high 50% range). This is better than the 1.2x DCF coverage ratio or 83% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry, per The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal.

I'm also as confident as ever that annual forward distribution growth will be near 5% in the years ahead. This is because Co-CEO Randy Fowler had encouraging closing remarks during the Q2 2025 Earnings Call.

He committed to between $2 billion and $2.5 billion in capex for the next couple of years. At that rate, EPD would have even more capital for more generous distribution raises and bolt-on acquisitions. Combined with all the projects that will be coming online over the next 12 to 18 months, Fowler had this to say about the prospects of returning more capital to investors:

But honestly, I think discretionary free cash flow is really about to take a step up in 2026, 2027, and that will give us an opportunity to come and return more capital to our investors. Co-CEO Randy Fowler

The Quant System agrees with this assessment. It anticipates 4.5% annual forward distribution growth for the foreseeable future, which would be in line with the sector median. That would represent a modest uptick from the annualized growth rate in recent quarters of around 4%. This is why the Quant System gives EPD a C+ grade for the forward distribution per unit growth metric (unchanged from May).

Risks To Consider

There's a lot to like about EPD. Yet, the partnership does have risks that could weigh on its investment thesis.

As I noted in my previous article, one of the key risks to EPD is counterparty risk. The vast majority (~90%) of the partnership's contracts are long-term and have escalation provisions. This builds a great deal of visibility into EPD's business.

However, the risk remains that a sharp and prolonged crash in energy commodities could have hurt EPD. That's because some customers may not remain solvent enough to meet financial obligations to the company. Additionally, such an event could also temporarily pressure customers to cut back on future exploration and production. This could also result in reduced growth potential for EPD.

Another risk to EPD is the potential for it to be the target of attempted cyber breaches. If any are materially successful, the company could face customer litigation and penalties from regulators.

One final risk to EPD is that it could encounter delays or cost overruns on major growth projects currently under construction. Given the more constructive regulatory environment and the company's outstanding track record, this is unlikely. Even so, it remains worth watching. Any major setbacks to project timelines could diminish EPD's growth outlook.

Summary: A No-Brainer Holding In My Portfolio

EPD's slew of growth projects set to come into service soon gives it a bright future ahead. The company's huge liquidity position and low leverage ratio afford it quite a bit of financial flexibility. The company also easily covers its distribution. Finally, units are priced below my fair value estimate. That's why I'm standing by my buy rating.

data.title: What AI Bubble? Alphabet's Business Is Booming
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The US Fed’s Jerome Powell saved the markets from what could have been a disastrous week, especially for tech stocks.

Markets opened for business last week with deep consternation about the possibility of an AI bubble bursting soon, compliments of commentary from OpenAI’s CEO Sam Altman. News of the AI bubble bursting got exacerbated after multiple reports suggested AI projects were not generating enough ROI, hitting tech stocks harder. Stocks would have fallen deeper in the red had Fed’s Powell hinted at rate cuts.

However, I view news of the AI bubble at the moment as just noise coinciding with several seasonal patterns of market weakness. During such times, investors should reassess the fundamental robustness of tech companies in the AI landscape.

In fact, Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) looks to be one of the strongest companies on the markets, gaining a deeper hold in its businesses involving AI and appearing set to outperform many of its hyperscaler peers. Yet the search giant trades at a very reasonable valuation.

I continue to recommend a Buy rating on Alphabet.

Alphabet Continues To Win Share In Major Markets

In April this year, I issued a Buy rating on Google, noting the company’s stock was the cheapest it's been in over a decade. This was despite my outlook for Alphabet to continue demonstrating strong performances across its businesses. More than 4 months later since my April coverage, I see Alphabet’s business has grown from strength to strength.

Alphabet is currently a $282.8B business growing at ~10% y/y on a TTM basis. After factoring in its latest Q2 CY25 report, I continue to see every single business segment of the search giant accelerating on a y/y basis.

While this year started off with deep concerns about whether Alphabet’s Google could hold on to its market share in the search market, which was being chipped at by OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Perplexity, Google is demonstrating market share winbacks based on an analysis of search traffic data. Google won back ~260 bp in market share, and I strongly believe Google Search’s AI Mode launched in March this year, along with the rapid global expansion of AI Overviews, has helped inflect Google’s Search business, as can be seen in Exhibit A above.

However, in my view, the biggest development in Alphabet’s fight back to stay dominant in the world of visionary technology comes in the resurgence in Alphabet’s cloud business, GCP (Google Cloud Platform). GCP has earned its place in Alphabet’s business segments as the fastest-growing segment, doubling the contributions GCP made to Alphabet’s revenue base, from accounting for ~7% of Alphabet’s revenues in CY20 to now accounting for over 13% of Alphabet’s revenues on a TTM basis.

The sustained rapid growth in Alphabet’s GCP explains why GCP won more market share in the cloud market versus its rivals, as I have illustrated below.

I am extremely encouraged by the deployments of products and features being launched at GCP, constantly pushing the barriers to innovation and creating real commercial opportunities for GCP to grow its revenue and earnings.

In fact, the latest report by Menlo Ventures shows that GCP has gained so much relevance among enterprise customers for its AI tools that Google’s share in the LLM market has skyrocketed to 20% from just 7% in 2023. In the meantime, OpenAI has lost half its share, falling from 50% in 2023 to 25% in the latest report. Moreover, Google Cloud also dominates over OpenAI in the market for AI code generation tools, AI’s first breakout use case for AI tools.

These developments in the AI market, especially for Alphabet’s GCP, are also coming at a time when enterprise spending is actually growing, contrary to what proponents of the AI bubble are purporting. Menlo’s report shows that enterprise spending for foundational LLMs has already more than 2x-ed to $8.4B, versus the $3.5B spent through CY24.

In my view, the noise about the AI bubble so far should be ignored, and investors should continue to focus on fundamentally sound businesses like Alphabet that are making positive developments in the markets they operate in. In Alphabet’s case, while Search is showing strong signs of inflecting, GCP is becoming a dominant force for Alphabet.

The appeal in Google Cloud is so strong that one of Alphabet’s direct peers, Meta Platforms (META), chose to sign a $10B deal with Alphabet’s GCP, which reinforces two things. First, enterprise spending on the cloud is here to stay, at least based on current signals. Two, Alphabet is poised to become a big winner of the AI race with business booming, unlike what markets were expecting.

Alphabet’s Valuation Is Still Cheap

I believe markets are waking up to the fact that they may have been unfairly punishing Alphabet with the noise about its various businesses. Until Alphabet’s Q2 earnings call, its shares were just about breaking even, up 1% for the year, far underperforming peers like Meta Platforms, Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN).

But the Q2 performance may change things around. Here’s how Alphabet’s stock is trending versus its peers since Alphabet’s Q2 results in July.

(Mind you, that is accounting for all the AI bubble news from last week.)

Despite Alphabet’s outperformance versus its peers over the past month, I still believe the company’s shares trade at an appealing forward earnings multiple of 21x.

Markets now expect Alphabet to grow its CY25 earnings by ~23.4% y/y to $9.9 per share on revenues growing 12.6% y/y to $394B. I see that the expectations for Alphabet’s EPS have improved by an additional 10.7%, with markets now adjusting their expectations for Alphabet to grow its CY25 earnings by 23% to $9.9 per share on revenues growing 13% y/y to $394B.

That is a meaningful improvement in expectations for which I believe Alphabet’s shares must be valued at a minimum of 24x forward earnings. Compared to the current valuation of 21x, that implies a 14% upside in Alphabet’s shares with a PT of $236.3 per share.

Risks & Other Factors To Consider

I am still assuming enterprise spending for AI and Cloud platforms will continue, at least at the current pace for Alphabet to keep growing its business. While I do not see any signals yet for AI spending to fall off a cliff, Alphabet’s outlook can be severely re-rated if spending drops. In addition, the other risk is that Alphabet again succumbs to competitive forces, which could cause headwinds in the market share gains Alphabet has demonstrated, as noted in this post.

Takeaway

I expect Alphabet to continue ramping up its efforts to build on market share wins it has seen this year. I view the talk of the AI bubble only as opportunistic and anecdotal without any strong data signals that point to the pop of the AI bubble.

Given all these data points in my observation, Alphabet’s stock is still a Buy per my analysis, and the valuation premium still points to further upside in Alphabet’s stock.
